On 12 March I finally reached a human agent, Daniel, who confirmed there was an underlying tariff misallocation and opened ticket 112233, promising to correct it and waive any late fees. That ticket was auto-closed by the system on 15 March with no action taken, and an engineer appointment that I arranged for 18 March was cancelled because the system showed a cleared balance. The cancellation meant I missed a critical installation at a client site, resulting in lost revenue of £480 and a need to rearrange staff and transport at short notice.

Over three subsequent calls (15 March, 20 March, 22 March) I have had to explain the same sequence each time. The chatbot provided inconsistent instructions, failed to escalate when prompted, and auto-closed legitimate human-raised tickets. I need a full manual audit of all interactions related to invoice March-360, written confirmation of the corrected bill, reimbursement for the missed installation (£480), and a named point of contact who will manage this case to completion. Please treat this as a priority and provide the requested audit and contact details within five working days so I can plan operations accordingly.

I am a pensioner and I need this sorted. My latest bill dated 18 March 2026 shows a direct debit of £64.99 for a broadband plan I have never signed up to. I have phoned your helpline four times over the past three weeks — on 2 March I spoke with Sarah Dixon who promised a corrected bill; on 10 March I spoke to Mark Phillips who said the change would appear on the next statement; and on 17 March I spoke with customer services (reference 48-173-329) and was told a supervisor would ring me the following day. No one rang. I cannot follow complicated online chats and I rely on simple, correct bills. This overcharge is reducing my bank balance and causing real worry. Please reverse the unauthorised charge, provide a corrected statement covering March and February, and confirm by post or by telephone to Mrs Anne Fletcher that the matter has been resolved within five working days. If you require any further information I will not be able to use an automated system — please assign a named advisor who will call me back at the number on file.

How charming that my termination charge of £420 turned up like a late birthday present. I cancelled my line on 05 March 2026 because the service has been unreliable for months. Each time I rang — Claire in support, then Sam in billing, then the online agent who "checked the account" — I was told the cancellation would be processed with no fee. Now you want to charge me the remainder of a 24‑month contract. I have been running a small consultancy; timing matters and I gave notice. If the contract still exists, provide the exact contractual clause and calculation showing how you arrived at £420, including the dates and the pro rata method. If you cannot, remove the fee and confirm in writing to my business email by close of business in five days. I have already run out of patience with runaround explanations. Take responsibility or I will take this to the Independent Ombudsman for Communications.

I switched from my previous provider on 1 March 2026 because your package promised faster home broadband for my home office and streaming. The engineer, Tom Reid, came on 3 March and set everything up. Since then the connection has been unusably slow for what you sold me: your speed checker on 7 March showed 14–18Mbps when I signed up to an 80Mbps plan. I ran the test again on 10 March at different times of day, same result. I did not expect perfection, but I did expect what was advertised. I have rearranged a couple of minor meetings because of buffering and my patience is gone. This is the first time I’m asking you to look into it. Please explain why the line is so far below the advertised tier and outline what you will do and when. I can wait a little while for the fix, but I need a clear explanation and a timetable — not vague promises.

I have been with your company since 2007 and I am extremely disappointed with the repeated failure to carry out a promised repair. My account (A/C 44-1100) has been without reliable service since 28 February. You booked an engineer for 01/03 (ref ENG-7321) who did not attend. I was told on 08/03 that an engineer would visit between 09:00–13:00 on 10/03 (ref ENG-7398); no one arrived and no explanation was offered. On 17/03 a senior advisor, Matthew P., guaranteed a resolution within 48 hours and to credit my account for the downtime; neither action occurred. The ongoing lack of service has disrupted my ability to work from home and leaves me unable to contact family in an emergency. Legally, under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 I am entitled to a repair or reduction in price; your repeated non-performance constitutes breach. I require a firm date for an on-site visit within 7 days, immediate interim measures to restore phone access, and a full schedule of compensation for the period without service. If you do not comply I will pursue this with Ofcom and consider a claim in the small claims court.

My name is Margaret Byrne and I'm calling about two unauthorised purchases on my bill totalling £86.50 on 12 March and 14 March. I am not confident with phones or apps and did not authorise these transactions; I noticed them when my daughter helped me check the account on 16 March. I rang customer services on 18 March and spoke with Sam in billing who said it would be looked into, and I also used web chat on 20 March where an advisor confirmed a fraud investigation had started. I have now been told different things and the matter has not been resolved, so I am asking for this to be escalated to a manager or for guidance on contacting the ombudsman if necessary. Please refund the two charges while you investigate and confirm the steps you will take to prevent this happening again to someone who is not comfortable with online security.

Account: 7D31

Called last week about 40-minute hold times. No resolution. Switched to you from BrightWave on 05/02/2026 and expected better. Please call back within 48 hours with a fix or I will revert. My time is limited.

Oh good, another surprise charge. The bill dated 15 March 2026 includes a "service administration" line for £6.49 which, surprise, was never mentioned when I signed up. I called on 18/03 and spoke to Sophie who promised an itemised breakdown and a refund if it was an error — nothing arrived. I'd love to be convinced this is legitimate, but since you apparently enjoy adding hidden extras to monthly statements, could you either explain this clearly with timestamps and consent evidence or credit my account? I expect an actual resolution rather than being fobbed off.

After twelve years as a customer I would have expected the sale process to be straightforward. On 6 April I agreed an upgrade to the 200Mbps package but the agent completed the order for the lower 20Mbps plan and added an optional insurance product I did not request. I raised this with your team on 8 April and was told it would be fixed; that has not happened and my bill reflects the wrong package. I am happy for you to correct the order to the originally quoted upgrade and remove the unsolicited insurance, and I would appreciate confirmation that the correct price will be applied to my account from the next billing period. A minimal note of disappointment — please resolve this promptly.

Account ending 2371. I have been a customer for over ten years and attempted to cancel my broadband on 4 March 2026; that request was not actioned. I called once before on 20 February 2026 to say I wished to leave. Please process the cancellation and confirm the final chargeable date by return. If you cannot complete this, provide the name and contact details of the person authorised to do so. I expect written confirmation within five working days.

I am writing to escalate a mid-contract price increase applied to my account. I entered an 18-month family plan on 1 January 2025 with a fixed monthly fee of £52. On 1 March 2026 my direct debit was increased to £58 without prior agreement from me. I have contacted customer services on four occasions (10 February — spoke with Raj, 25 February — web chat with Lisa, 4 March — phone to retention, 11 March — email) and each time I was given an explanation that the increase was due to rising wholesale costs, but no contract clause or specific calculation was provided to justify changing my monthly fee mid-contract.

I have now exhausted the normal channels and therefore request immediate escalation to a manager for review. Please supply (a) the contractual basis on which a mid-term increase is permitted, quoting the relevant clause, (b) a full breakdown of the calculation that raised my monthly charge from £52 to £58, and (c) confirmation of any compensation or refund for the difference charged while this matter is reviewed. If you cannot resolve this directly, please confirm how I should proceed with the Ombudsman Service. I would prefer to settle this with you, so please respond with a named contact and a clear timescale for resolution.

I purchased a handset through your online store on 25 November 2025 (order number 554321) and the device has been malfunctioning for several weeks. The model is the Epsilon 12, IMEI 356789012345678. Symptoms began on 6 February: intermittent screen flicker and accelerated battery drain; by 12 March the battery had swollen slightly and the device no longer charged reliably. I have contacted technical support via live chat (3 February), phone (10 February) and again on 5 March; the store team I visited on 12 March confirmed the fault and advised an RMA would be raised, but nothing has arrived and my last reference (SR-2026-03-12) has shown no updates. I have exhausted the normal channels and would now like this escalated to a manager or, if necessary, the ombudsman process. My request is straightforward: either a like-for-like replacement or a repair under the manufacturer’s warranty. Please confirm receipt of this escalation and provide a firm timeframe and case owner within five working days so I can plan accordingly. I appreciate that this is not an emergency, but I need a reliable handset for work and would like the matter resolved without further delay.

I moved over to your network from TalkFast on 02-Feb-2026 and accepted a handset deal at the same time. The phone arrived 09-Feb but began misbehaving within days: battery drops from 100% to 35% in under an hour, the microphone cuts out on calls, and the touchscreen intermittently fails. I opened a support ticket (45892) on 12-Feb, spoke with Aisha on 19-Feb who suggested a factory reset, and then phoned again on 26-Feb after the problems persisted. On 04-Mar I was told a replacement would be sent; nothing arrived and when I chased on 11-Mar I was given a different story and another reference number (46901). I am not new to smartphones but I am new to you, and I expected at least one straightforward fix rather than bouncing between advisors. The handset is effectively unusable for work calls and I am frustrated that my attempts to get a working device have required more time than the phone has lasted. Either replace the faulty unit immediately or provide a refund so I can buy a reliable handset elsewhere. I have repeated this complaint three times over the last month; please resolve it without further delay.

I am contacting you about the notice dated 15 March 2026 advising a contract price increase from £28.00 to £34.00 per month mid-term. I signed the fixed-term agreement on 12 January 2026 and there was no explicit clause permitting unilateral increases of this magnitude without my consent. I have a long-standing medical condition that restricts my working hours and income; although this is not an emergency service disruption, the extra cost presents a real hardship. Please confirm, in writing within 14 days, the contractual basis on which you rely to raise the fee and the specific clause in the terms and conditions that authorises this change. If you cannot point to an express, signed consent or a clear contractual provision, I will treat this as a unilateral variation and reserve the right to terminate the agreement without penalty and to seek redress under the Consumer Rights Act 2015. I expect a substantive reply rather than a templated message.

I have been a customer of your network for over a decade and I am contacting you because the automated chat assistant has repeatedly failed to address what should be a straightforward enquiry. I first tried the chatbot on 02 March 2026 to request clarification about recurring voicemail charges, received an unhelpful scripted response asking me to restart my device, and was then directed to an FAQ that did not cover the charge. I tried again on 09 March and 16 March; on each occasion the bot cycled through the same sequence and terminated the session with an invitation to call instead. On 23 March I finally managed to reach a human agent, Sophia, who noted the issue and said a specialist would follow up within five working days — I have not received that follow-up.

To be precise: the problem is not the charge itself but the support path. The conversational agent repeatedly requests irrelevant information, fails to acknowledge previous session IDs (chat IDs CH-02032026 and CH-09032026), and gives instructions that are not applicable to my device. This has led to multiple repetitions of basic account data and wasted time. I am requesting that this matter be escalated to a human advisor in the billing team, that the bot transcripts for the above chat IDs be reviewed for training improvement, and that I receive a clear timeline for when a specialist will contact me. I would also welcome a short explanation of what measures you will take to prevent similar loops for long-standing customers. I am of course willing to provide further details if required, but I would prefer a single, substantive response rather than another automated exchange.

I have a hearing impairment and I rely on text-based support. On 9 March I attempted to use your online chatbot to top up my PAYG plan and the interaction repeatedly demanded voice verification and provided no option to escalate to a human by text. I raised the matter once before on 11 March via your web form (reference 2139) and received only an automated acknowledgement. Under the Equality Act 2010 you are obliged to make reasonable adjustments to communication methods. Your current chatbot failing to provide accessible routes is not merely inconvenient; it is discriminatory in effect and has caused me unnecessary distress because I could not confirm essential credit for scheduled health messages. I do not require immediate emergency intervention; I require a substantive reply that explains how you will ensure accessible, non-voice support channels and what reasonable adjustments you will apply to my account. If you continue to respond with automated messages, I will escalate the matter to the relevant advocacy services and regulatory bodies.

I run a small catering business and we have relied on your business line and broadband for taking bookings and processing payments. I noticed on my invoices since January 2026 that my monthly fee has increased by £10 compared with the renewal offer shown on your website for new customers, effectively a loyalty penalty of £30 to date. As a commercial customer I expect transparent and fair pricing. I request an immediate adjustment to match the comparable new-customer tariff, and reimbursement for the overcharge for the past three months totalling £90. Please confirm the correction and any backdated credit within 10 business days, or provide the details I need to take the matter to the communications ombudsman for small businesses.

Hi — I run a small design studio and I rang to cancel my line on 05 March because we’re moving premises. I spoke with Tom and was given case reference 789456; I was told the cancellation would be completed within 48 hours and I’d get a confirmation email. Nothing has happened. The service is still active and I’m being charged. I rely on this for day-to-day admin, but this is just frustrating and avoidable. Please action the cancellation to be effective from 01 April and send written confirmation. If someone needs extra info, call me on the account number linked to this email; I don’t want another runaround.

Samantha Brooks, account holder for Family Plus 100 (Account 77‑9983). Your contract states expected speeds up to 100Mbps (typical evening speed 60–80Mbps), yet my household has been consistently receiving 8–12Mbps for the past three weeks. I recorded speed tests on 8 March (9.2Mbps), 12 March (11.1Mbps) and 19 March (8.5Mbps). I reported this on 16 March (ref 55902) and spoke with Ethan, who advised a series of router resets and updated firmware; those actions were completed but have had no effect. With working parents and children doing remote schoolwork, the service level is inadequate for a family plan. This is my second formal request — please arrange a proper line diagnostic and an on‑site engineer visit within the next ten working days, or provide a temporary upgrade at no extra cost while you resolve full throughput. I expect written confirmation of the remedial steps and a firm appointment date.

I am a student on a tight budget and I have just received my March statement with a charge I do not understand. My tariff is the Student Flex 8GB plan at £7.50 per month; my bill dated 15 March 2026 shows an additional fee of £11.99 listed as "service admin charge" and a separate VAT entry that brings the total unexpected amount to £14.39. I did not receive prior notification of any change to my plan or any new fee, and I have not used roaming or premium services that would justify this. This is the first time I have raised an issue with you about billing; I emailed accounts once yesterday, 24 March, and received an automated response only. As a student, an extra £14.39 makes a real difference to my finances. I request an immediate refund of the £11.99 charge and a full written explanation of what the charge covers and why I was not informed in advance. Please respond within seven days; if you cannot resolve this I will need to escalate the matter to Citizens Advice and consider a formal complaint to Ofcom.

I took out your Unlimited Sim plan on 12 February 2026 after comparing tariffs online; the sign-up flow was quick and I assumed the monthly price was all-in. My first bill, dated 5 March 2026, shows an extra line labelled "provisioning charge" of £12.99 and an "equipment admin" fee of £4.50 — both items were not visible at checkout or in the email confirmation. I work in software and I read terms before I buy, so this caught me by surprise; I’m a little disappointed that the total monthly cost is higher than the advertised headline rate. This is the first time I’m raising it, and I would appreciate a clear breakdown of those items and whether they can be removed or refunded for this billing period. If there’s a known cause, please explain it plainly rather than directing me to obscure policy pages in the app.

After twelve years with your company I attempted to cancel one of my services and the process has been needlessly obstructive. I called on 20 March to terminate the line associated with my old home broadband service; the advisor (Sonia) confirmed cancellation and gave me reference CXL-2048, promising the direct debit would be stopped immediately. I was then charged on 23 March and, on calling back on 24 March, Mark in Customer Care insisted the service was still active and that no cancellation had been recorded. I was told a supervisor would call back; no call has been received.

This is an unacceptable handling of a straightforward request from a long-term customer. I have been given contradictory information twice and charged for a service I requested to end. I want immediate confirmation that the cancellation is processed, an immediate refund of the latest payment, and a written apology. If you cannot resolve this to my satisfaction please escalate to a manager and advise how to proceed with an Ombudsman referral. I expect a response within seven days.

I moved my entire household to your network on 02/02/2026 and, as a consumer who expressly relied on your pre‑contract assurances, I now require urgent remedial action. On 07/03/2026 my supplied handset — Samsung Galaxy S21, IMEI 356789012345678 — failed to power on and will not charge. I contacted customer services on 08/03/2026 (case ref 20260308‑A) and spoke with Leanne in technical support who logged the fault but refused to provide a temporary replacement handset or a repair timeframe. I have attempted every basic diagnostic suggested by your agents and the phone is irretrievable. This is not a minor inconvenience: my work and household management rely on this device; direct debits are set up through apps on that phone and I cannot access them, exposing me to financial penalties. Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 I am entitled to a remedy where goods are not of satisfactory quality, and your obligations under the Ofcom Code to provide clear and timely fixes are not being met. I have exhausted the front‑line channels and now request immediate escalation to a manager with authority to approve either a same‑day replacement handset or an immediate refund and cancellation of the handset contract. If you do not provide a remedy within 48 hours I will refer this matter to the Ombudsman and seek compensation for costs incurred as well as any fees charged to my account during this period. Please arrange a phone call from a senior manager today; do not send scripted troubleshooting steps. I expect confirmation of the proposed remedy and collection/dispatch details within two working days.

I signed a 24‑month SIM-only contract on 15 September 2024 at £18.00 per month. On viewing my billing statement on 4 March 2026 I noticed the monthly fee had been increased to £23.00 with no prior notification in the app or by email. As a student on a very tight budget, an unexpected increase of £5 a month makes a difference to whether I can cover essentials this term and I did not agree to any mid-contract rise when I took the plan.

Please can you supply the exact contractual clause you are relying on to implement this increase, copies of any correspondence you say was sent to me about the change, and a clear calculation showing how the new amount was derived. If the increase cannot be justified under my current terms, I would expect the rate to be reverted to £18.00 and any excess charged since 4 March 2026 to be refunded or credited back to my account. I can wait a short time for a full review but would appreciate acknowledgement of this enquiry within seven working days and a timeline for resolution.

It appears you renewed my plan on 1 March and charged my account £24.99 without asking; how considerate. I've called three times (3 March with Adam, 10 March with Leah and again on 17 March with a notes-only advisor), lodged chats and left the account flagged — none of which changed the outcome. I am a student living on a tight budget; this unexpected debit has caused me to juggle rent and food. I expect a full refund for the unauthorised renewal and confirmation that my contract has been cancelled and will not auto-renew again. Please resolve this properly rather than offering the same delayed responses over and over.

I switched my number from EE to your network on 02 March 2026. On 15 March 2026 I noticed several unauthorised premium-rate items on my first bill and an attempted SIM port that I did not request. I have already spoken to your agents Ms. Patel (18 March) and Mr. Ahmed (19 March) who assured me the account would be frozen — the charges remain and the port attempt was not reversed. This is unacceptable. Freeze my account, reverse all unauthorised transactions, and provide a full written account of how this breach occurred within 48 hours. I have exhausted the standard contact channels; if you do not escalate this to a manager and confirm referral to the Ombudsman immediately I will commence formal complaints and seek regulatory action for fraudulent activity and data exposure.

Oh good — your auto-renewal system works wonderfully when it takes money I never authorised. I moved over from ClearWave on 6 February and was explicitly told by the sales advisor (Raj) that my introductory term would not auto-renew. Imagine my surprise when £89.99 was taken from my account on 20 March 2026 for a new 12-month term. I phoned on 21 March, spoke to a billing clerk who said they'd 'look into it' and then nothing. I called again on 23 March and was told a refund would be processed; it wasn’t. Today I spoke to a supervisor, who could not explain why consent was not recorded. It is extraordinary to be billed for a service renewal I did not accept. Please reverse the charge immediately, cancel any supposed renewal, and confirm in writing that my account will not be placed on any automatic roll-over. I expect a straightforward refund and documentary proof of cancellation within five working days. If you need to check with your systems team, please do so now rather than keeping me on hold or passing me from person to person.

On 16 March I reported a complete broadband outage at my premises (ticket number ABC123). That call established the fault and an engineer visit was promised within 48 hours; the appointment window has passed and the service remains down. I run a small consultancy from this address and the loss of internet connectivity has prevented card payments and client video calls, creating an immediate threat to daily takings and to scheduled work commitments. I made a follow-up call on 19 March and was informed an engineer would be reassigned, but I have received no confirmation of a visit time and there has been no temporary provision of mobile broadband as an interim measure. I require an on-site engineer visit today or the provision of a temporary, business-grade connection that will allow transactions to continue until the permanent repair is completed. Please escalate this to your business fault team and provide a named contact and a guaranteed time for resolution. A written statement of the compensation you will apply for business interruption would also be appreciated.

I switched from another provider and ordered the handset from you on 14 March (Order REF: ORD-49321). The phone arrived on 18 March and has been unusable from day one. The screen flickers intermittently, the battery drains from 100% to 15% within three hours with minimal use, the device reboots during calls, and the camera freezes. IMEI 356789012345678 if you need it. I have not contacted your support before now because I assumed a new device would be fine. I work full-time and rely on my phone for client calls; this is disrupting my day-to-day and cost me a meeting on 19 March.

I expect either a replacement handset of the same specification or a full refund. Please advise the process and confirm a timescale — I need a working phone within five working days. If you cannot resolve this promptly I will return the device and escalate through consumer channels. I paid for a functioning handset. I expect you to act accordingly.

I am writing because my broadband has been consistently slower than the package I pay for and I need assistance that is accessible and straightforward. I subscribe to the 30Mb package but speed checks performed in my home using the router’s diagnostics on 5 March, 12 March and 19 March 2026 repeatedly show download speeds in the range 2–5Mb and frequent drops in connection. I have contacted your support on four separate occasions (telephone 5 March — advisor Neil, 9 March — advisor Paula, 14 March — technical team, and 20 March — supervisor Helen). Each time I have been asked to run tests and restart equipment, which I am able to do, but the underlying problem remains and I am not confident performing more advanced troubleshooting. Given my limited familiarity with technology I request an engineer visit at a mutually convenient time, simple written instructions about any changes made, and a clear explanation if the service at my address cannot meet the advertised speed. I would appreciate a timely appointment within the next week and a confirmation call rather than further automated messages.

I moved into a shared house while studying and thought signing up with your student discount would be straightforward. It has not been. I’m Liam Patel, student, account 99214, and my broadband has been intermittent since 02 February 2026. The story: on 03 February I phoned and an agent called Mark promised an engineer would attend between 8am and 12pm the next Monday; the engineer never arrived. Mark said he would ring back. He did not. On 10 February I phoned again and a different advisor, Claire, booked a new visit for 17 February and said the fault would be fixed then. On 17 February an engineer called me to say the issue was with external cabling and that a third party needed to be scheduled; he left without completing any work and promised a follow-up. No one followed up. I spent two weeks using my phone as a hotspot and spent over £60 of my limited budget topping up data. I have deadlines and an exam on 18 March for which I need stable internet to submit coursework. When I phoned on 05 March to complain I was promised a credit for the period of outage and a guaranteed engineer slot within 48 hours; neither materialised. Each promise has been a promise kept only by your script, not by action. I am paying full student rates for intermittent service and have lost time and money because of your broken commitments. This is not a request; it is a demand for a manager to review the sequence of missed appointments, for an immediate onsite engineer visit before 48 hours, for the agreed credit to be applied to my bill, and for a written apology sent to my university email. If you cannot open a dependable line before my exam I will escalate to the Ombudsman and seek reimbursement for the extra data charges and any resubmission penalties I incur. I do not have the luxury of waiting for another empty promise.

Hello — I’m phoning back because the signal at my bungalow is still patchy. I am requesting a named manager to review the account and contact me directly within 48 hours with a plan to restore the number, clear the incorrect charges, and outline steps you will take to prevent a recurrence. Wonderful timing to switch off 3G, clearly. My fallback handset only works on 3G and it is the device I use for my personal alarm and to contact my carers. Since your network change on 20 March I have no reliable means to call for help. I cannot be expected to run around buying a new phone while I have mobility issues and a care routine that depends on an emergency line that now won’t connect. I phoned your automated line this morning and it just told me to ‘upgrade’ — as if I have the time or money to do that. This is not a convenience problem. If I am unable to reach emergency services because of your upgrade, that is a life-risk. Fix this immediately: provide a temporary 4G/VoLTE-capable device, or restore emergency connectivity to my number today, and send a technician to my address on 26 March. I expect someone competent to call me back before noon.

I have been a customer for just over twelve years and I am setting out the sequence of events because it illustrates why the current billing is both unexpected and unacceptable. In late January 2026 I decided to cancel my home broadband and mobile line after moving to a new address where another provider could give faster service. On 01/02/2026 I called retention and was given a cancellation reference (cancel-010226) and told the return box for equipment would be sent within three working days. The kit was collected by courier and a receipt was emailed to me on 08/02/2026.

Despite that, I was billed on 28/02/2026 for a further month’s broadband fee of £45 and then received a final demand for £90 on 14/03/2026. Over the past six weeks I have chased this matter on four separate occasions: call with Daniel on 03/02, email to billing on 12/02, follow-up with Priya in complaints on 25/02 and a voicemail left on 10/03. Each interaction ended with assurances that the charges would be reversed; none were. I have kept the cancellation reference and the courier receipt and can forward them. After more than a decade of business together I am surprised to be dealing with repeated billing errors and the time spent chasing them.

I am asking for an immediate reversal of the incorrect charges, written confirmation that my account is closed, and an explanation of why the billing system continued to bill following confirmed cancellation. I am willing to discuss this by phone but prefer written confirmation of the outcome. If this is not resolved promptly I will have to consider further steps.

Since switching to your service on 1 February 2026 I have repeatedly been charged fees that were not disclosed during the sale. On my first bill dated 28 February there is an unexpected 'activation fee' of £29.99 and a 'network admin charge' of £12.50; neither was mentioned in the online checkout or by the sales representative. I have contacted customer services on 3 occasions (5 March, 11 March and 19 March 2026) and asked for itemised justification; each time I was promised a full breakdown and then received no adequate response. Under the Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013 you are obliged to provide clear information about such charges at the point of contract. I therefore request a full refund of the undisclosed charges, a corrected final bill, and a written explanation of how these charges were allowed to appear. If I do not receive a substantive reply and refund within 14 days I will consider making a formal complaint to the appropriate regulator and seeking redress through the relevant consumer channels.

I am writing to report an instance of fraudulent activity linked to my account and to request formal escalation. On 10 March I received a call purporting to be from your security team and was persuaded to disclose a one‑time passcode; within 24 hours my account was used to transfer £320 to an unknown payee. I have already spoken to your fraud team on 11 March (case ref 76219) and again on 15 March when I was told the matter would be investigated. Both advisors — Hannah in fraud and Raj in customer services — acknowledged the unauthorised transaction but no refund or firm outcome has been provided and I have been advised that ordinary channels are exhausted. As an elderly customer who is not confident with online banking or complex passwords, this has left me without a reliable line and without the disputed funds. Please escalate this matter to a manager immediately and confirm whether you will refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman; I expect a written response within seven working days setting out your proposed restitution and the steps you will take to prevent further misuse of my account.

I am 72 and not very good with smartphones, but I can tell when the service is failing. For the past three months, calls drop and 4G disappears in my bungalow on Foxglove Close, BN3 8RT. I have called support on 4 occasions (12 Feb, 3 Mar, 17 Mar, 31 Mar) and spoke with advisors called Raj and Leah who advised network checks and a new SIM. Neither suggestion has helped. Please arrange a site check or provide a credible plan to improve signal at my address. If a signal booster or alternative solution is available, confirm eligibility and lead time. I appreciate there may be technical constraints, but repeated standard troubleshooting has not fixed this and I would like a clear next step within 10 working days.

I bought a handset from your online shop at the start of term because I could not afford anything more expensive and I rely on it for coursework, placement coordination and for calling university welfare if needed. On 2 February 2026 the screen began to flicker and on 6 February it failed to boot. I waited three days for a courier both times. On 12 March I spoke to Hannah in technical support who told me a local depot had the replacement but it would not be dispatched until I confirmed my address; I confirmed it immediately and received no further update.

Narratively, the effect has been cumulative: without reliable phone access I missed a supervisor’s urgent message about a placement change on 9 March and could not submit a lab report on time because the only device I possess is the faulty handset. I have limited funds and cannot buy another phone to bridge this gap. I need either an immediate working replacement handset dispatched by next-business-day courier or a temporary loan phone so I can meet my academic obligations and contact support services as required. I will await written confirmation of dispatch within 24 hours; otherwise I will have to pursue other consumer remedies.

Bought an iPhone 13 Pro on 15/01/2026 via your UpgradePlus 24 plan (order ref UP-99876, IMEI 356789012345678). The microphone has been cutting out during calls since 07/03/2026 — people say I sound faint or the call drops. I’ve spoken to support on 08/03, 12/03 and again via chat on 17/03; each time I was given a different workaround and then moved to another team. I depend on the handset for client calls during the day, so this is disrupting my work. I want either a warranty replacement or a full refund and a supervisor to approve the request: escalate this to a manager and provide the Ombudsman contact details if you cannot resolve it in 5 working days.

This is a formal complaint following a previous report. I am the account holder for the family plan (FamilyShare 4-line, account FS-447760). On 10/03/2026 I spoke with Sophie in Technical Support who assured me an engineer would attend my property on 17/03/2026 to remedy recurring broadband outages and the failure of our home router to obtain a stable sync. No engineer arrived and service has continued to fail: intermittent broadband has disrupted my children’s remote schooling and my partner’s ability to work from home. Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 and the terms set out in your service description, I am entitled to a repair within a reasonable time or an adequate remedy. I request that you (a) arrange a guaranteed engineer visit within seven calendar days, (b) apply a proportionate service credit for lost availability since 01/03/2026, and (c) confirm in writing the escalation path should the issue remain unresolved. I raised this matter once before; having had no substantive action, I must insist on a timely and documented resolution. Contact me at the mobile ending 2204 or by email: claire.morton@icloud.com.

I moved my broadband and mobile services to your network on 05 February 2026 and expected a straightforward billing transition. My first bill, dated 08 March 2026, includes a so-called "network administration fee" of £39.99 and a "setup charge" of £45 which were not disclosed when I signed up online. I raised this once on 16 March 2026 with an agent called Maya who said the charges would be reviewed, but my account still shows the same amounts and I have had no follow-up. Please provide an itemised explanation for these fees and a refund or credit for any charges that were not made clear during the sale. I would like this resolved within seven days; if you need further details I can supply my order number and the confirmation email from 05/02/2026.

I am writing as the account holder and I am not confident with online systems. On 12 March 2026 an engineer was scheduled to upgrade my line but did not attend and I received no call to explain. I have not previously complained and am now requesting a clear written explanation, a copy of any records relating to the missed appointment, and a new confirmed appointment within seven days. Please note your statutory obligations under consumer protection law and your published complaints procedures; if you cannot provide the requested information I will consider referring the matter to the appropriate regulator.

I am contacting you regarding excessive waiting times when trying to reach support. As the account holder for our Family Plan (Account 8745612) I need this matter addressed promptly. On 18 March and again on 22 March I attempted to call your support line to add a data bolt-on for my teenage son. Both times the hold exceeded 40 minutes before I gave up. My wife also tried on 23 March and was on hold for nearly an hour.

This is not a casual annoyance. I work full time and schedule calls during lunchtime; holding for this long is disruptive and inefficient. I have not spoken to any advisor about these incidents — this is my first formal notification — but the pattern over four calls in one week is unacceptable for a paid service.

Please confirm within five working days what steps you will take to reduce call-waiting times and how you will compensate for the time lost. I expect a clear timeline for improvement and a direct contact point so future calls are handled more promptly.

I run a small catering business and rely on your mobile service for orders and supplier calls. On 9 March I raised this issue by email: my subscription auto-renewed on 1 March without my explicit consent and a charge of £129.99 was taken from the business account. I called on 11 March and was told a refund would be processed; the refund has not appeared. This is my second attempt to resolve the matter. I would like the auto-renewal cancelled, the charge refunded, and confirmation that future renewals will require explicit consent. Please respond with the steps you will take and an estimated timescale for the refund.

I am a paying customer who reads the small print. I signed up to your 'Flex Pro' tariff on 3 January 2026 because the promotional page promised unlimited calls and data and no additional fees beyond the monthly rate. My first bill, dated 1 February 2026, includes a mobile protection fee of 7.99 and a so-called 'service admin charge' of 4.50. Neither were disclosed at sign-up. I have combed your terms and the app UI: the only reference is an obscure clause buried in a PDF called Terms v3.2, which is not the version linked on the sign-up page. I phoned billing on 10 February (agent name: Claire) who said the charges were legitimate and pointed me to the same PDF. I then used live chat on 17 February and spoke to Mark, who said he would escalate; nothing happened. I have kept screenshots, the signup page, and the exact timestamps of each call and chat. This is not an oversight. As someone who works in software I can say with certainty that fees that are hidden in mismatch documentation are effectively undisclosed. I demand an itemised explanation of each extra charge, a refund of anything not explicitly agreed at signup, correction of the online sign-up flow so future customers are not misled, and to speak with a manager or have this referred to the Ombudsman if it is not resolved within 14 days. I am not interested in being fobbed off with automated messages; provide evidence or remove the charges.

I switched my account to your service on 01/02/2026. On 15/02/2026 I was presented with an early termination charge of £420 for two lines that I reasonably believed had been out-of-contract with my previous operator. I have raised this matter formally on 20/02, 27/02 and 10/03 and have repeatedly requested a full breakdown of the alleged outstanding sums. Pursuant to the Consumer Rights Act 2015 and Ofcom’s guidance on charges on transfer, I require a written itemisation of the calculation within 14 days and immediate suspension of any recovery action pending review. If you maintain the fee, please set out the contractual provision you say authorises the charge, provide copies of the relevant signed agreements from the prior supplier, and confirm your intended method of collection. I shall consider referring this dispute to ADR if you do not comply with a statutory-level explanation and waiver where charges are not properly substantiated.

Invoice dated 15 March shows an unexpected administrative charge of £65 described as 'set-up fee' that is not in our contract. I run Greenfield Café and depend on your connection for card payments and ordering; this charge puts cashflow at risk. This is my first formal contact about the matter. Remove the fee, issue a full refund, and confirm in writing within seven days. I expect a prompt commercial resolution; otherwise I will switch suppliers and recover losses.

I have called your helpline three times in the last month — on 2 March, 9 March and most recently on 16 March — to report the same problem: my phone simply loses signal in my sitting room. Apparently my postcode (RG6 5BX) is not of interest to the network, because every evening my calls drop and texts take hours to arrive. The last agent, Phil, suggested I move to a different room; helpful, but not exactly a long-term fix for someone who prefers to stay seated. I can manage voicemail and my landline, but relying on being able to use the mobile for appointments and shopping is sensible. I was told twice that an engineer visit would be scheduled; no date has been given. I suppose I could take up residence by the back door if that helps your coverage statistics, but realistically I need a practical solution: either improve the indoor signal or offer a temporary alternative while you investigate. I would like confirmation of the next steps and a clear timeframe.

Following up on the issue I raised on 18 March (ticket 788412): my March invoice shows a £97.50 data overage that does not match my usage. My app shows I am on the 50GB plan and the usage graph records around 18GB for the billing period. I submitted screenshots via the app but have had no update. I need this corrected before the direct debit is taken tomorrow as I cannot accept a charge that appears to be incorrect. Please suspend the payment, reverse the disputed charge and confirm the corrected amount in writing within 24 hours. I can provide log files if required; I expect a technical resolution promptly.

When the first outage happened on 18 March I assumed it would be resolved quickly — after all, I have been a customer for more than a decade and I depend on your line for my home office and evenings. The sequence is as follows: I noticed intermittent drops at 09:30 on 18 March and reported it via the app at 11:02; an automated message confirmed a fault ticket would be opened. At 14:00 a case handler called to say an engineer would attend between 09:00 and 13:00 on 20 March. No engineer arrived and I received no update. On 21 March I phoned and was told the engineer had left feedback but that a follow-up would come within 48 hours. That did not happen. Today, 25 March, I am still without a reliable connection at certain points in the day and have had to move calls to my mobile. I am writing to request a full status update: what the investigation has found, why the scheduled visit did not take place, and a concrete appointment window for a site visit. A single clear timeline would be helpful; repeated silence is causing avoidable disruption to my work schedule.

I have been a customer with your company for over a decade and I am disappointed by the lack of progress on an issue I have raised repeatedly. Timeline: automatic renewal charge of £48 for my "Home Fibre Plus" plan was taken on 04/03/2026. I first called on 06/03 and spoke with Aaron (ref 54122) who said the payment would be reversed. I called again on 13/03 (spoke to Marisol, ref 55401) and was told the refund was 'in the system' and to allow up to 14 days. After no movement I called a third time on 22/03 and was given a different explanation that the renewal was processed because my payment details were saved. I did not consent to an automatic renewal; my preference has always been to review offers before committing. Given my 10+ years with you I find this handling unsatisfactory. What I want: a full refund of the £48 renewal charge, confirmation in writing that auto-renew has been disabled on my account, and an explanation of why two separate agents gave me conflicting information. I am not asking for immediate escalation to regulatory bodies, but I would like this resolved within two weeks and a note on my account so further errors are avoided. Thank you for addressing this properly.

I have been a customer since 2014 and I have a progressive mobility condition that makes budgeting and routine extremely important. On my most recent bill, dated 01 March 2026, I was charged £6 a month more than the rate I was given when I joined. I have checked your website and new customers are being offered a lower price for the same package. I phoned your general enquiries line this morning to ask why long-term customers are being charged more; your automated menu would not transfer me to accounts and the online tariff page is unclear. I need a full explanation of why my price has increased, why there was no individual notice explaining this change, and an immediate review to match the advertised new-customer rate back to March 1st. I rely on a fixed income and occasional home care; small, unexplained increases matter. This feels like a penalty for staying loyal. I am asking you to correct this, refund the difference from 01/03/2026 and confirm in writing what my ongoing monthly charge will be. If you need to contact me, call my landline — I prefer not to use online chat because of my condition. This is my first formal contact about the matter but I will escalate if I do not receive a clear, reasonable response within 14 days.

Mrs Margaret Ellis here. I am elderly and not confident with technology; on 02 March 2026 I accepted what I understood to be a simple SIM-only plan over the phone. The adviser who handled the call added an additional data bundle and extended the contract term without explaining implications, resulting in an extra £15.50 per month on my bill. I raised this once with customer services on 10 March 2026 and spoke to Rebecca, who said the matter would be corrected and I would receive a confirmation letter. No correction has been made and the additional charge appears on my most recent statement. Please resolve this matter promptly and, given my circumstances, provide a clear written explanation in plain language and a telephone contact to confirm completion. If you cannot correct the billing within five working days, please advise how I may escalate to a manager or the Ombudsman.

Hi — I'm Tom Li, and my mobile data has been intermittently dropping for the past two weeks. Please provide a technical status update, the expected resolution timeline, and whether this will be prioritised. I do not like having to chase this repeatedly. I can wait a few days, but I cannot keep risking missed orders while I am told someone else will do the job that never gets done.

I am a young professional working from home and I am reporting persistent poor mobile coverage at my address, postcode E2 8AA, which has affected my ability to meet professional obligations. My name is Elsie Morgan. I have been on your 'Golden Home' broadband package for several years. On the bill dated 12 March 2026 there is a "loyalty surcharge" of £3.50 applied for the first time. I am not good with technology and noticed a text on 14 March saying my account had been used from a new location. I did not authorise any change and I am reporting this now for the first time. I would appreciate clear, slow instructions — I do not want to be moved between departments.

I switched my household services to you on 15 February 2026. On 28 February I submitted a formal complaint by email concerning repeated billing notices and an unexplained suspension warning (reference COMP-20260228). Hi — I run a small bakery in Brighton (The Flour Pot) and your phone and internet have been down since Saturday 20/03/2026. No card payments, no online orders, no emails. I’ve gone through all the normal routes and nothing has been fixed. I need someone who can actually restore service now. My staff are juggling cash only and I have suppliers asking why invoices aren’t being paid — this is jeopardising our relationship with a key supplier and our staff wages. Please put me straight through to a manager who can get a priority engineer out and confirm an ETA. If I do not have a working connection by close of business tomorrow I will have to request intervention from the ombudsman and seek compensation for lost income. This is urgent — my business cannot continue like this.

On 18 March I put my toddler in the buggy, dropped Milo at nursery and called to report an unexpectedly high direct debit; I was on hold for 58 minutes and missed the school pick-up because I couldn’t hang up. Two days later, 21 March, I queued again for a billing explanation and listened to hold music for 72 minutes only to have the call drop when someone finally answered — the agent, Emma, cut out after five minutes. This is not a single annoyance, it’s a pattern: I have tried webchat, email and phone and have yet to get a meaningful resolution. I manage the family plan and the delays are hitting logistics at home; please escalate this to a manager, explain what you will do to reduce these excessive waiting times, and let me have the complaints process and Ombudsman contact so I can decide whether to take this further if it isn’t fixed within four working days.

I have been with you for more than ten years. Today I am writing because of what I can only describe as a cascade of failures by your automated front line. At 14:07 on 23 March I started a chat session via the app, session ID 7c9a21b2 — the transcript shows the chatbot asking me to verify my postcode, then ignoring the verification and suggesting a full equipment reset. I provided a screenshot of the error message (voucher shows 10 days remaining, app display shows 0), and the bot responded with the same troubleshooting checklist three times in succession. At 14:23 I asked to speak to a human. The bot replied that an agent would be with me shortly, then closed the window and emailed me a canned 'we thought we solved your issue' note at 14:30. I called support at 15:05 and the agent, Marcus, confirmed he could see the transcript but could not access the diagnostic logs the bot referenced. He said he would escalate to Tier 2; the reference number he gave is 48291. This is my first formal complaint about the matter. I am not seeking immediate compensation — the fault is minor — but I am extremely frustrated by the lack of coherent handover between automated systems and human staff. The bot will not surface the correct information to agents, agents cannot access the logs the bot cites, and I am left repeating myself. Please investigate the session continuity problem, supply the Tier 2 update that Marcus promised, and outline the steps you will take to ensure that automated triage actually hands cases to humans where appropriate. I expect a technical response detailing session ID 7c9a21b2 and the diagnostic outcome, otherwise I will escalate through your formal complaints procedure.

I cancelled my contract on 01 February using the online portal and received confirmation email ref CNCL-20260201. Despite that, I have been billed for March and April: a direct debit on 05 March for £34.99 and another on 05 April for £34.99. I raised the issue with customer support on 06 April (agent Sarah, case 889004) and again on 12 April via the webchat where I was told the March payment would be refunded. That refund has not appeared, and I have just been notified by my bank that a late payment marker has been placed against my account because the system still shows an active service. I followed every required step to cancel and to return any hardware — I sent the router back on 04 February (tracking 3S123456789GB). Please reverse the charges, remove any adverse markers on my credit file, and provide a written confirmation to my email within 24 hours. If you cannot resolve this, forward the matter to a manager or the ombudsman so I can secure a prompt remedy.

I am a young professional and I raise this as a principled complaint about your reliance on an AI assistant that fails to satisfy basic consumer protections. On 10 March 2026 I attempted to resolve a billing query via the chatbot and the exchange produced inconsistent statements about contract terms and early-exit fees. I logged a ticket (REF: BIL-10032026) and was told a human would review, yet no substantive reply has been provided. Under the Consumer Rights Act and your published terms, customers are entitled to accurate explanations of charges and an auditable trail of communications. I request a written account of the chatbot interaction, an explanation of how its answers are validated against contractual terms, and the name and direct contact of a complaints officer who can provide a binding clarification. I am not asking for immediate escalation, only that you remedy the informational deficiency and confirm within ten working days how I may obtain a formal correction to my account if required.

Dear Customer Services,

I am contacting you as the account holder for Family Plan ID 77-438-900. I manage this mobile plan for my family (4 lines) and have been a customer for several years. On 03/03/2026 I queried my monthly bill with your adviser Hannah R. (call reference CRN-232903) who advised she would escalate because the plan price applied to my account, £38.99 per month, appears to be significantly higher than a promotional rate currently marketed to new customers (£26.99 per month) for the same ‘Together 50’ product.

This is my second attempt to obtain a satisfactory explanation. I raised the matter informally by telephone and was told it would be reviewed; no adjustment or further correspondence has been received. I am requesting a formal review of my account charges, a retrospective credit for any difference since 01/01/2026, and an assurance that my plan will be aligned with equivalent new-customer tariffs or an alternative loyalty offer. Please respond with your decision and calculations within 14 days. If you require further documentation, advise promptly so I can provide it. I expect a clear, professional resolution without further delay.

Yours sincerely,
Daniela Browne

I have exhausted your standard complaint channels and require escalation.

Account name: Sarah Malik. Mobile number: 07412 958034. I am a student on a limited income. On 18 February 2026 I phoned your support line and spoke with James Carter (call ref 18FEB-2203). He confirmed that my tariff would not increase until the end of my 12‑month fixed period but that the system showed a price rise effective 1 March. I raised this again with Customer Resolutions on 26 February (CR-9981) and received no substantive reply.

This appears to be a breach of the fixed-term pricing terms set out in clause 4.2 of my contract dated 02/03/2025. Your unilateral application of a higher rate before the term ends is not permitted without my explicit consent. I am therefore formally requesting: (a) immediate correction of my billing to reflect the contracted price from 01/03/2026; (b) refund of any overpayments; and (c) a written confirmation that my tariff will remain at the contracted rate for the remainder of the fixed term.

If you do not provide a satisfactory resolution within 14 calendar days of this message I will escalate to the independent adjudicator and pursue the matter through the Communications Ombudsman. I am attaching copies of the contract page, the two call references and the March invoice that shows the higher charge. Please transfer this matter to a senior manager for urgent review and confirm receipt and intended timetable for action by return.

As a customer since 2012, I expect accurate information and an auditable record of interactions. On 21 March 2026 I queried roaming charges using your chatbot and was given incorrect information about EU roaming allowances which, if acted upon, could have resulted in an avoidable charge. This is my first formal complaint about this matter. I require a written confirmation of the correct roaming terms applicable to my plan, the chat transcript from 21 March, and an explanation of what quality controls you use on AI responses. Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 and the General Data Protection Regulation, please provide the requested records and a substantive reply within 14 days. I am disappointed that automated advice appears unreliable and wish to be reassured that this will not recur.

I have been a customer since 2019, and the progression to this point feels like a book I didn't sign up to read. Freshers' term, 2019: I signed up because the student deal was reasonable and your staff promised loyalty would be recognised. Fast forward to 2026. Tuition fees have gone up; rent eats most of my grant; yet my monthly bill has grown while the adverts outside campus show new customers getting better deals. I first raised this with customer services on 02/03 (case 361-02-03) and spoke to Tom on 06/03 who said someone from retention would call. They did not. I have called back three times since — names I remember are Rhianna and Marcus — and each time I am put through hoops: re-verification, 'currently reviewing your account', suggestions to switch to a bundle I cannot afford, and automated offers that require me to transfer existing contract terms to a new plan. I have proof of better deals for new customers: the price gap is almost a fiver a month, which matters when I'm budgeting for food and books. I am not asking for charity. I am asking for fair treatment: either apply the best available student rate to my plan from next billing cycle, or give me a clear, penalty-free way to leave. Tell me which it will be and do it by 30 March, otherwise I will take this further with Citizens Advice and my university's student union.

Outage since 24/03/2026 07:15. All lines dead across the family plan; children cannot access remote school, no inbound calls possible. This is the first report. Please restore service immediately and confirm by return.

On 12 March 2026 your notice about the 3G switch went live and by 14 March my 3G-only landline alarm — the pendant my father wears — stopped sending alerts. He had a fall on 18 March and the control unit could not reach your network. I called support four times over the next two weeks (14 March, 18 March, 22 March and 28 March) and spoke to different advisors: first to Tom who logged a ticket, then to Aisha who said an engineer would call, then to two others who kept reclassifying it as a coverage issue.

Each time I was promised a workaround or an interim SIM; none arrived. The last note I was given was reference 87921 and an assurance the problem was 'being prioritised' — but nothing has changed and my father has already had to rely on neighbours for help. I switched providers precisely because I expected reliability for exactly this kind of vulnerable-senior scenario; instead I am now supervising his care more intensively and arranging paid alarms because your transition left him exposed.

This is the fifth call in as many weeks. I need a functioning connection for the alarm immediately: either a compatible replacement device, a temporary 4G-enabled unit, or urgent re-provisioning of his line within the next 24 hours. If you cannot resolve this today, please put me through to a duty manager who can arrange emergency provision and confirm in writing the steps you will take to prevent any further lapses. My father’s safety depends on you acting now.

I’m writing because this is no longer about a slow service; it’s become a recurring interruption to my life. I moved into student housing on 6 October and took your 50Mb package because you advertised stable speeds for people who need to study at home. From November through January the connection was patchy but usable. In February it fell apart. Lectures buffered, group calls dropped, and an online seminar on 14 February took 40 minutes to reconnect because the line kept dropping. I called on 15 February and spoke to Louise in broadband support who sent a signal check and promised an engineer within five working days. The engineer (called Sam) came on 22 February, replaced the filter and said the issue would clear. It did not. I called again on 1 March, then 12 March and again on 2 April. Different agents — Tom, Priya, an automated callback system — all logged faults, ran tests, and promised escalation. Each time I was told the problem was on the line or outside the property. On 9 April, after another call, I was told a priority escalation had been opened; I received an email the next day saying the case was closed with no fix. I am at the point of desperation. My coursework depends on being able to join live seminars. I can wait for a proper response but I cannot keep wasting hours on calls that lead nowhere. I want this sent to a manager for meaningful diagnostics, a concrete timetable for repair, and a record I can take to the ombudsman if you cannot meet that timetable. Please do not reply with another standard script that assumes the problem solved itself. State the steps you will take, who is assigned, and when they will attend the property.

I am writing with a detailed account of my inability to cancel the broadband package attached to account number 778-445. I have a chronic mobility condition which makes lengthy menu navigation and repeated phone calls difficult; I first requested cancellation on 6 January via the online chat and explained my circumstances to an advisor named Sara on 9 January. That request was refused without a clear reason, so I telephoned again on 20 January and spoke to a different agent, Mark, who said he would log a cancellation and transfer me to retention. On 3 February I received a bill for another month despite those assurances. I have exhausted the usual channels — online chat, telephone and the help centre — and have no straightforward way to complete this administrative task without assistance. Given my health needs and the repeated failures to cancel as requested, I am asking for this matter to be escalated to a manager and, if unresolved within a reasonable time, for information on how to involve the Ombudsman. I would prefer confirmation in writing of the cancellation date and any refund due to avoid further calls.

I’m contacting you again because long wait times on your phone line are causing me real difficulty. I have chronic back pain and panic disorder, so being on hold for ages makes symptoms worse; I’ve reached out four times already (27 Feb, 6 Mar, 12 Mar and 15 Mar) and yesterday I waited 1 hour 22 minutes before the call dropped. I asked for a callback option and was told it’d be logged, but I haven’t received one. This is now my fifth attempt and it’s exhausting both physically and mentally. I need a priority callback within 48 hours and an accessible contact route — email or an arranged phone window — to resolve the ongoing billing query (case references: 441209, 441312). Please also note this as a reasonable adjustment on my account and confirm a goodwill gesture for the repeated waits, as the delays have had a real impact on my ability to manage appointments and medication schedules.

I have been calling repeatedly for three weeks about an intermittent service issue and the lack of follow-through is affecting my wellbeing. My home broadband has been dropping completely several times daily since 02 February 2026; I recorded dropouts on 02/02, 07/02, 10/02 and 18/02. I have spoken to agents on four separate occasions: initial report on 03/02 with Sarah who logged a ticket (ref: BR-220302), a follow-up on 09/02 with Dave who scheduled an engineer for 12/02 that did not arrive, a voicemail left on 15/02 to Customer Care and a further call on 20/02 with Priya who said an escalation would happen that day. None of those promises materialised. I rely on a stable connection for telemedicine appointments related to my chronic condition and for automated alerts from my health monitoring equipment; the outages have caused missed consultations and considerable anxiety. As a vulnerable customer, I expected priority handling and clear timelines; instead I am being shuffled between representatives. I need a guaranteed engineer visit within 72 hours, confirmation of what diagnostics you will perform, and a concrete plan for remediation. I also request a pro rata credit for the affected period and clear assurance that future appointments will be honoured rather than repeatedly rescheduled. Please treat this as urgent — I have already called multiple times and cannot continue to wait without a firm commitment.

Account number 88765421. I switched from my previous provider on 15 February 2026. My first bill (dated 01 March 2026) shows a charge of £48.99 when my plan was advertised as £28.99. That is a £20 discrepancy. No discounts, no usage beyond allowance. This is my first time raising the issue. Please correct the bill, apply the proper rate, and confirm the corrected amount by email with a revised invoice.

I never thought a network change would leave me stranded, but here we are. I’m a student at the University of Leeds, living on a tight budget and juggling part-time shifts and coursework. I use my old pay-as-you-go phone for simple things — timetables, returning tutor calls, a cheap backup for travel. On 1 March I started noticing calls dropped and data that wouldn’t load. By 3 March my phone said there was no network for the 3G services I rely on. I called your support on 4 March and spoke to Ben who told me 3G was phased out and that my handset was unsupported; he suggested buying a 4G phone or switching to a more expensive tariff. I told him I couldn’t afford that right now; he logged the problem but gave no alternative. I sent an email on 9 March after missing a shift because my employer could not reach me. That email got a canned reply and no help. This is my second follow-up. The way this has been handled — no practical help, just being told to spend money I don’t have — is leaving me with fewer hours of paid work and real anxiety about how I’ll manage rent next month. I don’t want moralising about technology. I need a short-term solution: either enable basic voice and text on my current SIM for another 30 days, or provide me with a low-cost replacement device or SIM that works on your remaining networks at a reduced rate while I save. Please treat this as urgent; I need an answer in the next 72 hours so I can plan shifts and bills.

Well done — you’ve decided to increase my monthly rate halfway through the term of my Business Essential 100 contract. How helpful. My invoice for account BIZ-4402 was £60.00 last month; the latest bill dated 12 March 2026 shows £72.50 and a bland note about a ‘tariff adjustment’. Either honour the rate in my contract or give me a clear explanation, in writing, of the contractual clause you are relying on, together with the exact date the notification was sent to me. I trust you appreciate that repeatedly chasing you is a poor use of my time, and your current approach is unacceptable.

I suppose it is efficient that I have now reached the point of needing to ask for a complaints manager, given the flawless consistency of unhelpful updates. To be specific: a total outage on 3 March 2026 rendered our shop unable to process card payments for two days. I reported the outage and then began a rather educational tour of your contact channels. The ongoing inability to take payments has caused immediate cashflow issues and potential penalties for late supplier payments. I am therefore requesting that this be escalated without further delay to a senior complaints manager and that a copy of the full call record and the incident log be provided. If you cannot provide a definitive resolution within three working days, please advise how I should proceed to obtain independent review via the Ombudsman. I would prefer a prompt substantive outcome rather than further hold music.

Dear Sir/Madam,

If the charge stands, supply detailed logs demonstrating how the excess occurred. I expect a written response to this email within five working days. Oh good, another brilliant example of customer service in action — except it isn’t. Since 20 March my landline has been intermittent and completely unusable for emergency calls; I first reported it on 21 March and have been reassured by three different people since then: Jamal (21/03), Fiona (25/03) and Mike (28/03). For clarity: I depend on that line for my carer's scheduled check-ins. The silence from your end is not comforting. I would appreciate it if someone could perform an actual repair rather than a scripted apology. Please send an engineer today and confirm a realistic arrival window. I've been with you for over a decade so I must say I'm disappointed — the home broadband has been painfully slow for weeks. If you can't resolve it internally, I'd like this sent to your formal complaints manager or to the ombudsman to review. I expect a call back to agree next steps within seven working days.

My cafe's broadband and phone have been completely down since 11 March 2026, leaving us unable to process card payments or take orders. I am a student on a tight budget and have been experiencing persistent low broadband speeds for several weeks. I am willing to have a technician appointment and to replace the router if required, but I would appreciate a clear plan with dates, a confirmation of what tests you will perform, and a partial credit for the weeks where advertised speeds were not delivered. Please advise the next steps and propose available appointment times.

I am writing because the phone supplied to me on 01/03/2026 is failing in a way that creates a vulnerability for my health. Given my health condition, I require an expedited remedy. Please arrange an immediate replacement handset or same-day courier collection and delivery of a functioning device, and assign a dedicated advisor who understands that a delay is more than inconvenient — it is a safety risk. If you cannot meet this urgent requirement within 24 hours, please advise what alternative support you will provide so I am not left without emergency contact capability.

I switched to your service from BrightNet on 04 February 2026 and, regrettably, I was mis-sold a package during the onboarding call. When my first bill arrived on 03/03/2026 it showed activation of "HomePlus 65" with a first-month charge of £65 and a pro-rated early termination fee of £120 for my old provider. If this cannot be resolved by a senior manager within seven days, I will be asking the Ombudsman to investigate the mis-selling and undue charges. Please escalate without further delay.

I am a student dependent on a strict weekly budget and your chatbot has just cost me money. I finally reached a human adviser, Lucy, on 12 March who could not explain why I received no confirmation and advised me to dispute the charge via the app. I attempted that but received an automated reply and no resolution. Each interaction took time I cannot spare and has left me short for essential groceries this week. If you cannot resolve this within four working days, I will be forced to seek external complaint handling. Please provide a direct contact person and a timeline for crediting my account.

I want to tell you why this feels unfair. For twenty years I paid for the same broadband package and, because I live with chronic rheumatoid arthritis, predictable bills matter to me; I budget carefully. Last month I discovered that a new customer promotion offered the same speed for £10 less than I’m being charged. I raised this with an agent called Tom on 09 March and he promised someone would review my account; nothing happened. I’m not throwing accusations around — I’m explaining the situation: I stayed loyal through periods when my mobility made moving provider difficult, because I trusted you would value long-standing customers. Instead I feel penalised for staying put. I don’t want to sound confrontational, but I do want parity or an explanation of why returning customers are given better rates than those who stayed. Please review my account (account name: Elaine Turner, account number 77-1984) and tell me what you can offer that recognises my circumstances. If there is a reason I’m being charged more, explain it clearly and plainly — I had to call a taxi to get to a shop once because of flare-ups, I shouldn’t now be paying a premium for the convenience I relied on for years. I raised this once before and was told someone would call back; I’m still waiting.

I run a small catering business and noticed my annual plan renewed on 20 March without any prior reminder; my card was charged £299. I hadn’t been expecting an automatic renewal this month and didn’t give consent for an early charge. I’m raising this now to request a refund of the renewal fee and confirmation that the service will be cancelled unless I explicitly re-subscribe. I rely on predictable cashflow, so a prompt reversal within a few working days would be helpful while I decide whether to re-sign.

Funny how 'replacement handset dispatched' on 02/03 turned into three weeks of niceties and nothing helpful. I am registered as disabled and depend on the phone for daily health check-ins. I called on 05/03 and spoke to Sharon who said an urgent replacement would be sent. Then I waited. I have called again on 12/03 and 18/03; each time different promises, same outcome. The replacement (model GX42) arrived with a broken power button. The handset fails while I am on medication reminders. I appreciate that mistakes happen. If you cannot do that, say so plainly and let me take an alternative. I am tired of being soothed with 'we're looking into it'.

I've lost count of calls — I first rang on 05/04 and then again on 12/04, 20/04 and most recently 02/05 — and still you keep sneaking charges onto my StreamMax 50 bill that were never disclosed when I signed up. The line item 'Network Admin' for £12.50 and a mysterious 'service activation' of £35 turned up on April's invoice; neither was mentioned in your online checkout, the T&Cs, or on the confirmation email I received on 01/04. I've spoken to Sam in billing (02/05), who put me on hold for 25 minutes and promised a call back that never arrived, and to Liam on chat who said the items were valid because "system default settings apply" — which is not an explanation. I'm tech-literate, I've read the tariff page front to back, and this feels like deliberate obfuscation. Please provide an itemised breakdown, reverse the £47.50 of unexpected fees by Friday 08/05, and confirm in writing that you will stop adding these hidden charges to future bills. If you can't sort this quickly I will cancel and take this further, but I'd rather not have to do that.

Hi — first time I'm reporting this and I'm annoyed. On 22 March 2026 my number (07xxxxxx123) went dead for no reason and I then got an SMS saying my SIM had been transferred. I have been with you for over a decade — since April 2013 — and I am extremely disappointed to find my monthly line rental increased without proper notice.
